name: Karna
description: Karna, also known as Vasusena, Radheya, and Angaraja, is a prominent and complex figure in the Hindu epic Mahabharata. Born to Kunti before her marriage to Pandu, Karna was abandoned and raised by a charioteer and his wife. He is renowned for his exceptional martial prowess, particularly his skill in archery, and his unwavering adherence to dharma, even when faced with morally ambiguous situations. Despite being denied his rightful Kshatriya status, Karna remained steadfast in his loyalty to Duryodhana, the Kaurava prince, and fought valiantly on their side during the Kurukshetra War. His story is a poignant exploration of fate, social injustice, and the complexities of duty, loyalty, and identity. Karna's tragic life and ultimate sacrifice continue to resonate in Hindu culture, making him a compelling symbol of generosity, honor, and the challenges of living in a world marked by inequality and predetermination. His character embodies the tensions between birth and merit, highlighting the societal biases of the time. He is often viewed as a tragic hero, a victim of circumstance and societal prejudice.
subtitle: The Son of Surya

keyMyths.title: Karna's Birth and Abandonment
keyMyths.description: Karna was born to Kunti after she invoked a mantra and received Surya, the sun god, as her divine lover. He was born with golden armor and earrings, making him invulnerable. Fearing societal repercussions, Kunti abandoned him in a basket on the river. He was then found and raised by Adhiratha, a charioteer, and his wife Radha, hence the name Radheya. This abandonment shaped his destiny and fueled his later struggles with identity and social standing. The circumstances of his birth contributed to the prejudice he faced throughout his life, leading to his alliance with Duryodhana.

keyMyths.title: Karna's Acquisition of the Brahmastra
keyMyths.description: Desperate to prove his worth and overcome his perceived low status, Karna sought training from Parashurama, a renowned Brahmin warrior and guru. Karna falsely claimed to be a Brahmin in order to be accepted as a student. He learned advanced martial arts, including the Brahmastra, a powerful celestial weapon. However, Parashurama later discovered Karna's true identity and cursed him, stating that he would forget how to use the Brahmastra at the moment he needed it most. This curse played a crucial role in Karna's eventual defeat.

keyMyths.title: Kunti Reveals Karna's Identity
keyMyths.description: Before the Kurukshetra War, Kunti approached Karna and revealed that he was her eldest son and the half-brother of the Pandavas. She urged him to join their side, promising him the kingdom. Karna, bound by his loyalty to Duryodhana, refused to betray his friend, even though he knew the truth. He promised Kunti that he would not kill any of the Pandavas except Arjuna, thus ensuring that she would still have five sons after the war, regardless of its outcome. This encounter underscores Karna's complex moral compass and his unwavering commitment to dharma.
